title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Haberdasher suggests that Poirot advise the group on recent events and recommends calling the police, sparking an animated debate among the students. Genevieve protests the idea of police involvement, while Nigel Chapman and Patricia Lane defend Nigel against accusations of theft. During this discussion, Poirot presents Sally Finch with a pair of evening shoes, explaining his deduction that her missing shoe was taken to the Baker Street station lost and found. He reasons that a thief would likely hide a single shoe on public transport during rush hour rather than attempting to destroy or sell it. Noting the agitation of Len Bateson and the other students, Poirot states he will only discuss the "nuisance" if the group invites him to do so. After Archibald Bletchley suggests a democratic vote, Sally Finch and Nigel urge Poirot to speak. Poirot then officially recommends that Mrs. Haberdasher or Mrs. Nicoletti call the police immediately.

Mrs. Haberdasher escorts Poirot to a private sitting room, where she expresses anxiety and agrees with his recommendation, though she wishes he had been more discreet to avoid a scandal. Their conversation is interrupted by Colin McNaughton, a graduate student in psychiatry and psychology. Colin criticizes Poirot’s methods as outdated and narrow, arguing that crime should be understood through psychological motivation rather than legal guilt and punishment. After a brief disagreement with Mrs. Haberdasher, Colin begins to explain his perspective to Poirot, using the theft of Sally Finch's shoe as an example of a "Cinderella complex."
